data: Two joining fragments preserving the upper left portion of the text (top left corner is intact); the right hand fragment is more poorly preserved with the surface more degraded. The rear of the stone is very rough and uneven.
Remains of two lines of Latin letters across the stone, with traces of a third line in Greek below

Thinly incised letters with terminal points/serifs to the strokes. Greek letters are much smaller than Latin letters. The M tends lightly towards the cursive form, and some letters join at the foot; A in the Latin text has an upward sloping cross-bar; Greek text appears to have lunate epsilon.
1st century or beginning of 2nd century CE (Korhonen)
Observed by Mommsen in the Collezione Biscari. Formerly in sala IX.89, now in magazzino superiore
Photo J. Prag courtesy Museo Civico di Catania
Castello Ursino, Piazza Federico II di Svevia, 95121 Catania (CT)
Inscriptiones Graecae Siciliae et Italiae, additis graecis Galliae Hispaniae, Britanniae, Germaniae inscriptionibus

G. Kaibel, Inscriptiones Graecae Siciliae et Italiae, additis graecis Galliae Hispaniae, Britanniae, Germaniae inscriptionibus, Inscriptiones Graecae consilio et auctoritate Academiae Litterarum Regiae Borussicae Editae. Volumen XIV., XIV (Berlin: Georgius Reimerus, 1890)

T. Mommsen, Inscriptiones Bruttiorum Lucaniae Campaniae Siciliae Sardiniae Latinae. Pars posterior. Inscriptiones Siciliae et Sardiniae, Corpus Inscriptionum Latinarum, consilio et auctoritate Academiae Litterarum Regiae Borussicae editum, 10.2 (Berlin: G. Reimer, 1883)
La Sicilia da Sesto Pompeo a Diocleziano
Giacomo Manganaro, «La Sicilia da Sesto Pompeo a Diocleziano», Aufstieg und Niedergang der romischen Welt 2.11.1 (1988): 3–89
Le iscrizioni del Museo civico di Catania : storia delle collezioni, cultura epigrafica, edizione
Kalle Korhonen, Le iscrizioni del Museo civico di Catania : storia delle collezioni, cultura epigrafica, edizione (Helsinki: Societas Scientiarum Fennica, 2004)

ISic000361. Fragmentary bilingual epitaph for Marylla

Text encoding: Marta Fogagnolo

2vixit · ann(is) [L---]

3Μ(αρκία) Μάρυλλα̣ ἔζ̣[ησεν]
4ἔτη ν[---]´

Text encoding: Marta Fogagnolo
section a
1·M·MARYLLA
2VIXIT·ANN[.---]
section b
3ΜΜΑΡΥΛΛ.Ε.[....]
4ΕΤΗΝ[---]

Facsimile Images

Apparatus criticus

  • Text based on photographs. The Greek fragment has not been observed: Mommsen was the last editor to observe it. ;
  • line.2: Mommsen, Kaibel: ann(os); Korhonen: ann(is)
  • line.3: Mommsen read Μ·ΜΑΡΥΛΜ·ΕΖ[---]. On the surviving fragment (a) are visible the top of an Α followed by a lunate Ε and the top of a Ζ.

English translation

Winnie Smith

Marcia Marylla lived 5- years. Markia Marulla lived 5- years.

Italian translation

Marta Fogagnolo

Marcia Marylla visse 5- anni. Markia Marylla visse 5- anni.

commentary

Provenance is uncertain, but likely Catania (for another such bilingual compare IMusCat 74 = ISic000348). The Greek text is now almost wholly lost, partially reported by Mommsen in CIL 10, no.7078. Mommsen was unable to read the name on line 3 in full, but the traces printed in CIL appear entirely compatible with the name in line 1. The surviving traces of line 3 on the left-hand fragment are clearly compatible with Α ΕΖ (the epsilon being lunate). Korhonen observed that the visible traces were seemingly not compatible with ΡΥ, but failed to recognise, seemingly, that they are in fact compatible with letters later in the same line, and that the letters of line 3 are therefore much smaller than those in lines 1-2.

The name Μάρυλλα is found in another inscription from Catania (IG 14, no.465 l. 2 = ISic001289: see LGPN 3A: 289) and in the masculine form in an inscription from Megara Iblea (Arena 4). According to Korhonen, Marcia is the gentilicium or praenomen of the woman.

bibliography

  • Mommsen 1883 7078 https://biblio.inscriptiones.org/epig10000589
  • Kaibel 1890 491 https://biblio.inscriptiones.org/epig10001072
  • Manganaro 1988 49 n. 243 https://biblio.inscriptiones.org/epig10001427
  • Korhonen 2004 109 https://biblio.inscriptiones.org/epig10000028

Thinly incised letters with terminal points/serifs to the strokes. Greek letters are much smaller than Latin letters. The M tends lightly towards the cursive form, and some letters join at the foot; A in the Latin text has an upward sloping cross-bar; Greek text appears to have lunate epsilon.

Lines 1-2 30-35 Interlineation line 1 to 2 not recorded

· Marcia · Marylla vixit · annis L
Μαρκία Μάρυλλα ἔζησεν ἔτη ν
Text based on photographs. The Greek fragment has not been observed: Mommsen was the last editor to observe it. Mommsen, Kaibel: ann(os); Korhonen: ann(is) Mommsen read Μ·ΜΑΡΥΛΜ·ΕΖ[---]. On the surviving fragment (a) are visible the top of an Α followed by a lunate Ε and the top of a Ζ.

Marcia Marylla lived 5- years. Markia Marulla lived 5- years.

Marcia Marylla visse 5- anni. Markia Marylla visse 5- anni.

Provenance is uncertain, but likely Catania (for another such bilingual compare IMusCat 74 = ISic000348). The Greek text is now almost wholly lost, partially reported by Mommsen in CIL 10, no.7078. Mommsen was unable to read the name on line 3 in full, but the traces printed in CIL appear entirely compatible with the name in line 1. The surviving traces of line 3 on the left-hand fragment are clearly compatible with Α ΕΖ (the epsilon being lunate). Korhonen observed that the visible traces were seemingly not compatible with ΡΥ, but failed to recognise, seemingly, that they are in fact compatible with letters later in the same line, and that the letters of line 3 are therefore much smaller than those in lines 1-2.

The name Μάρυλλα is found in another inscription from Catania (IG 14, no.465 l. 2 = ISic001289: see LGPN 3A: 289) and in the masculine form in an inscription from Megara Iblea (Arena 4). According to Korhonen, Marcia is the gentilicium or praenomen of the woman.
